description: Study 5: adversarial false-claim ablation. 4 problems x 2 variants (true/false stated answer) x 3 forced conditions (forced_visual = plot+Read; forced_textual = SymPy sample points; forced_reflection = one prose paragraph, no tools) x ~2 runs = 48 cells.
motivation: Finally exercise the corrective mode (the pilot's decisive use case) and ablate WHICH activity - visual, numeric, or a bare pause - catches a subtly false stated claim.
formal_results: False claims caught at indistinguishable rates: forced_textual 8/8, forced_visual 7/8, forced_reflection 7/8 (the 2 misses are operational - a transcript-parse failure and a timeout). No cell that emitted a verdict ever sided with the false claim. In forced_visual cells the verdict grounds in the tool's NUMERIC metadata (e.g. 0.2399 matches sqrt(pi)*e^-2, not the claimed sqrt(pi)*e^-1), never a visual property read by eye.
results_description: All three forced modalities catch false claims about equally; visualization holds no advantage, and the picture is ignored in favor of cheaper numeric metadata.
conclusions: Modality doesn't matter; the forced verification step does. Visualization is sufficient but not necessary - the 'viz is uniquely corrective' hypothesis is refuted.

comments.correction: A long-form draft line ('the agent never looked at the pictures') was wrong about the CAUSE - images WERE delivered inline after math-viz commit 1622bac; the negative conclusion (verdicts ground in numeric metadata, not visual properties) is unaffected/strengthened. Per CLAUDE.md pending corrections.
